title: LNG Canada Export Terminal
companies: Shell, Petronas, PetroChina, Mitsubishi Corporation and KOGAS; Shell - Shell Canada (“Shell”) is a subsidiary of Netherlands-based Royal Dutch Shell plc (RDS); full ownership info here. Petronas - Petroleum Nasional or National Petroleum, based in Malaysia, owned by Malaysian govt; Petrochina -  holdings - acc to Wikipedia, Chinese oil and gas company and is the listed arm of state-owned China National Petroleum Corporation (CNPC), headquartered in Dongcheng District, Beijing; Mitsubishi Corporation is a Japanese integrated trading and investment company that develops and operates businesses across multiple industries together with its global network; KOGAS is a Korean natural gas company.
summary: Kitimat; doubling production of liquefied natural gas to 14 million tonnes annually, according to reports. From Woodward: "Beyond the significant climate risks — LNG terminals in British Columbia are meant to export gas from the Montney Play — a globally recognized  “carbon bomb” — there are economic risks. The global market for gas faces a major glut — with a fivefold increase in supply coming online over the next three years. Researchers at the Institute for Energy Economic and Financial Analysis question who will purchase the gas that could flood the market; LNG Canada recently began exporting liquefied natural gas at its terminal in Kitimat, BC, representing the country’s first LNG export terminal, and to date, the largest private sector investment  in Canadian history. The company’s owners (Shell, Petronas, PetroChina, Mitsubishi and the Korean Gas Corporation) have plans to expand the site, known as Phase 2, and a final investment decision is expected this year."
permitting_stage: Pre-Construction Stage: Front end Design and Engineering Contract awarded as of Sept. 2025; Impact Assessment issued 2022 - Reviewed as changes to initial impact assessment of LNG Canada facility; Certificate Issued by BCER June 18 2015 by BCER. See press announcement.
commodity: LNG Export: Primairly North-East Asian markets, China, Japan, South Korea
indigenous_perspective: In 2015, according to BCER final decision, "Haisla Nation has entered into an impact management and benefit agreement with the Proponent." According to Reuters, "own a 50.1% equity stake in the $4-billion Cedar LNG export project near the town of Kitimat, British Columbia. Calgary-based Pembina Pipeline(PPL.TO), opens new tab owns the remainder." + "One crucial component was a 2018 deal that gave the Haisla access to 400 million cubic feet per day of capacity on the 670-km Coastal GasLink pipeline that brings natural gas to the larger Shell-led (SHEL.L), opens new tab LNG Canada facility."
analysis: According to one report: LNG Canada has yet to make a final investment decision on Phase 2 SO this designation could be signaling to banks and private equity firms that the project is being prioritized by federal govt, i.e. derisking; on Aug. 1, the company awarded a new engineering contract to the JGC-Fluor joint venture—the same team behind Phase 1; The project already hold key permits and approvals, including the provincial environmental assessment certificate. G&M reports that, "LNG Canada’s co-owners are expected to make a final investment decision in 2026 on whether to forge ahead with a Phase 2 expansion, which would double the plant’s total capacity to 28 million tonnes a year."
financing: LNG PHASE 1 already received contributions through the Strategic Innovation Fund and exemptions from steel import tariffs

title: Darlington New Nuclear Project
location: Michi Saagiig Anishinaabeg, Williams Treaties, Gunshot Treaties, Williams Treaties Settlement Agreement
companies: OPG (ON Power Generator), Canada Growth Fund Inc., Building Ontario Fund
summary: In Clarington. Criticism: "An independent study released last week by the Ontario Clean Air Alliance found that the Darlington SMRs will cost up to eight times as much as onshore wind, almost six times as much as utility-scale solar, and 2.7 times as much offshore wind in the Great Lakes after factoring in the federal tax credit. The analysis by Hinesburg, Vermont-based Energy Futures Group “used data from Ontario’s Independent Electricity System Operator (IESO) but used realistic real-world capital costs and performance measures to develop a more accurate comparison of the cost of nuclear and renewable power options,” OCAA writes."
permitting_stage: Pre-Construction Stage: Preparation license approved in 2021; April 4 2025 - Construction approval - "Commission’s decision to issue a power reactor construction licence to Ontario Power Generation Inc. (OPG) to construct 1 General Electric Hitachi BWRX-300 reactor." May 8 2025 - Provincial approval. Federal approval obtained one month earlier by Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission.
commodity: Nuclear Energy: Ontario Grid
jobs: 18,000 jobs; OPG: "Citing Conference Board of Canada figures, OPG said the four SMRs will contribute $38.5 billion to Canada’s GDP over 65 years and sustain an average of about 3,700 jobs per year, including 18,000 per year during construction."

title: Contrecoeur Terminal Container Project
companies: Montreal Port Authority, DP World (U.A.E) opperations; Built by private contractors: "The contract, worth $609 million, for all water works, including the listed preparatory work, has been awarded to the CTCGP consortium (Contrecœur Terminal Constructors Grand Project), composed of Pomerleau (60 per cent) and Aecon (40 per cent)." Acc. to G&M: "Global logistics giant DP World Ltd. has been tapped to build and run the Port of Montreal’s new Contrecoeur container terminal."
summary: https://www.port-montreal.com/en/the-port-of-montreal/projects/terminal-in-contrecoeur
permitting_stage: Pre-Construction Stage:  October 9, 2025, the Port of Montreal began preparatory work for the expansion and awarded the contract for in-water works of the future container terminal to the CTCGP consortium; Construction contract issues Sept 8 2025: "Port Authority announced it finalized and submitted an application to the Department of Fisheries and Oceans for a permit required to complete the in-water work, part of the environmental assessment process. It expects to receive a response from federal authorities in the coming weeks." March 1 2021, fed EA approved with 330 conditions.
commodity: Container Shipping: Europe and Asia Markets, primairly raw goods, new facility meant to accomodate mineral/gas resources
analysis: Critique: https://www.winnipegfreepress.com/arts-and-life/life/greenpage/2025/10/26/residents-say-port-of-montreal-expansion-in-contrecoeur-unnecessary-harmful
financing: Acc. to G&M: The final financing package for Contrecoeur has yet to be hammered out. Ottawa and Quebec have already pledged $150-million and $130-million respectively toward the estimated $1.6-billion project, pitched as a key piece of transportation infrastructure needed to serve markets in Quebec, Ontario and the U.S. Midwest. The Canada Infrastructure Bank has also committed a $300-million loan.
jobs: 8,000 jobs during construction, 1,200 jobs during operation

title: McIlvenna Bay Foran Copper Mine Project
companies: McIlvenna Bay Operating Ltd, subsidiary of Foran Mining Companies. Largest shareholders of Foran: U.S.: Agnico Eagle Mines (17%); Canada: Fairfax (17%); CAN: Public sector pension investment (13%)
summary: Proposing the construction, operation, decommissioning and abandonment of a base and precious metals underground mine and surface mineral processing facility located 65 kilometres southwest of Creighton, Saskatchewan. As proposed, the project would produce copper and zinc ore concentrates containing gold and silver up to a total production rate of 4,200 tonnes per day. Concentrate products from the processing plant are proposed to be transported by truck to a third-party rail loading facility to be distributed for smelting. The lifespan of the project is expected to be up to 40 years.; From company website: "With Probable Mineral Reserves of 29.7 Mt containing 793 million pounds of copper and 1.4 billion pounds of zinc included in a Mineral Resource of 39 million Indicated tonnes grading 2.02% CuEq for 1.0 billion pounds of copper and 1.9 billion pounds of zinc and 5 million Inferred tonnes grading 1.8% CuEq for 93 million pounds of copper and 260 million pounds of zinc."
permitting_stage: Construction Stage: 56% complete as of Sept. 2025; July 24, 2023: "ministerial approval under Section 15 of the Environmental Assessment Act was granted to the McIlvenna Bay Project." Northern Miner reports that the fast track was for feasibilty study, but what's this? And this?
commodity: Critical Minerals: Copper, Zinc, Gold, Silver;
indigenous_perspective: Collaboration Agreement Signed between Foran and Peter Ballantyne Cree Nation (2023); Metis Nation letter to Canada re whether it meets the definition for a designated project under the federal EA.
financing: According to Northern Miner: Earlier this month, Prime Minister Mark Carney included the feasibility-stage McIlvenna Bay on a fast-track list for projects of national significance under the Major Projects Office. Foran has also drawn the interest of Agnico Eagle Mines (TSX, NYSE: AEM), which in May increased its stake in the company to 13.5% by investing $90 million, as well as a $350 million private placement that included $156 million from the federally owned Canada Growth Fund."

title: Red Chris Porphyry Copper-Gold Project
companies: Newmont, Imperial Metals Corporation
summary: From BCER: "The Red Chris Porphyry Copper-Gold Mine (Red Chris) is an open pit mining operation that produces copper, gold, and silver. The Red Chris Environmental Assessment Certificate (Certificate) is held by Newcrest Red Chris Mining Limited (NRCML), a wholly owned indirect subsidiary of Newmont Corporation (Newmont). Red Chris is located in northwest British Columbia, approximately 80 kilometres south of Dease Lake and 18 kilometres south of the Village of Iskut. Red Chris received Certificate #M05-02 in 2005 and has been operational since 2015. There have been five amendments to the Certificate since it was issued."
permitting_stage: Certification Issued ; see also. Feds actually play zero role here, but as CBC explains, this is about a signal the feds are sending about "transforming this part of BC."
commodity: Critical Minerals: Copper; Asia-Pacific trading partners
indigenous_perspective: Acc to BC: "Under a consent agreement between the Tahltan Central Government (TCG) and the Province, TCG is carrying out its own assessment and must also consent to the project." From Red Chris: "NRCML manages Red Chris in close partnership with Tahltan Nation through an Impact Benefit and Co-Management Agreement (IBCA).  The IBCA ensures that Tahltan expertise, perspectives, and values guide the operation. Since production began in 2015, Red Chris has become a key economic pillar in Tahltan territory. The mine employs approximately 220 Tahltans and generates approximately $100 million annually in business with the Tahltan Nation Development Corporation (TNDC).  In addition, the mine pays annual royalties to the Tahltan Heritage Trust and contributes mineral tax revenue to the Province of British Columbia (BC) which is shared directly with the Tahltan Nation."
analysis: Backdrop is disastrous Mount Polley tailings pond spill, owned by Imperial Metals and its ongoing aftermath.

title: Sisson Mine
companies: Northcliff Resources Ltd., Todd Minerals Ltd.; According to Sisson website: Northcliff Resources Ltd., 88.5% controlling interest; remaining 11.5% owned by Todd Minerals Ltd., a subsidiary of the Todd Corporation of New Zealand.
summary: Acc. to CBC, Sisson is a mine that is getting $$ from US DOD: "an announcement by Northcliff Resources in early May that it was awarded $20.7 million from the U.S. Defence Department and a conditional $8.2 million from the Canada government to help develop tungsten production at the Sisson Mine project north of Fredericton."
permitting_stage: Pre-Construction Stage: Certification issued by feds in 2017. Source: New Brunswick Environmental Impact Assessment (2015); Federal Environmental Impact Assessment (2017); Metals and Diamond Mining Effluent Regulations Schedule 2 Amendment (MDMER) authorisation (2019);  Sisson Fisheries Act Authorisation and Off-setting/Fish Habitat Compensation Plan (2020); Construction commencement timeline extended to December 2025 (2022); EIA Completed in 2016 and 2017
commodity: Critical Minerals: Tungsten, Molybdenum; North American, European and Asian markets
analysis: Outdated EIA
jobs: 300 Permanent Employees (2013 estimate to be updated in 2026)

title: Crawford Nickel Project
companies: Canada Nickel, Taykwa Tagamou Nation (TTN)
summary: Canada Nickel Company is proposing the construction, operation, decommissioning and abandonment of an open-pit nickel-cobalt mine and on-site metal mill, located 42 kilometres north of Timmins, Ontario.
permitting_stage: Planning Stage: Completing Stage 2 of the federal permitting process; Impact Assessment in progress; Construction to begin 2026
commodity: Critical Minerals: Nickle, lithium, graphite, cobalt, copper and rare earth elements; Export market to North America
indigenous_perspective: IBAs being developed; three FNs have "inside track bidding" for contracts
analysis: Two factors are potentially interesting to analyze: (1) the claim that this is the second-largest nickel reserve globally and (2) the company's claim that "Tailings and waste rock from ultramafic nickel deposits can store CO2—Crawford’s feasibility study includes the ability to store 1.5 million tonnes annually, making it a net negative contributor to the global carbon footprint"
promises: $70B to Canada's GDP over 40+ years; $16 billion in fed / prov tax revenue; $16 billion in job income (1,000 direct and 3,000 indirect jobs)

title: Ksi Lisims
location: Nisga'a Treaty territory, and Gitga’at First Nation, Gitxaała Nation, Kitselas First Nation, Kitsumkalum First Nation, Lax Kw’alaams Band, Metlakatla First Nation, Haida Nation, and Gitxsan Wilp Denimget, Tsihl Gwellii Laxwiiyip
companies: Western LNG (funded by Blackstone and Apollo Global Management), Total Energies, Partnership with Nisga’a government and Rockies LNG. The PRGT pipeline is jointly owned by the Nisga'a government and Western LNG.The floating terminal will be built by Samsung Heavy Industries. Western LNG has an offtake agreement with Shell to purchase 2 million tonnes of LNG per year (Western LNG, 2024); Nisga'a has no equity stake, assets are entirely owned by Western LNG which is based in Texas and financed by private equity firms, with Blackstone providing the anchor investment.  More on US private equity ownership:  Furthermore, floating LNG terminals will be constructed by Samsung overseas.
summary: Ksi Lisims is a proposed floating LNG terminal on Pearse Island that will export LNG to Asian markets. It wil cost $10B and have an export capacity of 12 mt/year, which will make it the second largest LNG plant in Canada. It is connected to the contentious PRGT pipeline with an expired environmental certificate and sparking opposition from Gitanyow hereditary leadership, from Gitxsan youth and communities, and community members within Nisga’a. The PRGT pipeline is jointly owned by the Nisga'a government and Western LNG. As pointed towards by Gitanyow hereditary chiefs, Western LNG is financially backed by billionaire MAGA doners and others with ties to trump (stopprgt). Neither Ksi Lisims or LNG have a final investment decision yet.
permitting_stage: Pre-Construction Stage: Assessments completed and permits issued (2025); Certificate issued by BC EAO on Sep 15, 2025, after conducting the assessment on behalf of both provincial and federal governments. [Federal government released Impacted Assessment decision in Sept, 2025.]
commodity: LNG: Export LNG to Asia
indigenous_perspective: Consent from Nisga'a (which owns half of the pipeline project), Gitga’at First Nation, Kitselas First Nation. No decision from Gitxaala Nation. Kitsumkalum First Nation, Lax Kw’alaams Band, Metlakatla First Nation and Haida Nation state they do not consent. Source: BC EAO Reasons for Decision . Opposition: Gitanyow Hereditary Chiefs (filed for judicial review of BC EAO's ruling that they did not need to be consulted for LNG; ), and the pipeline project  is facing opposition from Gitanyow hereditary chiefs (including an IPCA declaration and the Flying Frogs camp), Gitxsan huwilps and community members (including a legal challenge and Dog Sanctuary a few dozen meters from the PRGT right of way). Recently, The Lax Kw’alaams Band and the Metlakatla First Nation filed separate judicial review applications in Federal Court for the LNG project's environmental approval. Protest :  The Lax’yip Firekeepers: http://laxyip.com/, Donations: E-transfers to firekeepers@laxyip.com; Wilp Aasosxw: http://laxyip.com/wilp-aasosxw, Donations: E-transfers to landbackcc@gmail.com; Flying Frogs - https://flyingfrogsawakened.org/, Donations: E-transfers to deborahgood3@gmail.com;
analysis: LNG is contentious because Nisga’a Nation champions it. Extractive projects are pitting nations against each other, undermining traditional diplomatic ties. As stated in a recent Yellowhead Institute summary report, "This pattern reflects broader systemic priorities: Indigenous economic participation is encouraged when it aligns with resource development, but when communities assert sovereignty over land or propose alternative economic models, they encounter resistance". In relation to the project, the report also notes several economic risks including the projected global LNG supply glut and risk of stranded assets. The authors also note that "At the time of writing, few long-term contracts are secured for shipping, requiring shippers to rely on volatile short-term contracts," posing a significant investment risk. Metlakatla First Nation alleged in its Oct. 14 court filing "that the decision relied on 'speculative economic concepts' to justify the project’s adverse impacts, while ignoring 'mounting evidence' that it is not economically feasible." The filing noted the two issues of a potential supply glut and lack of buyers, as well as its use of 'unproven and untested' floating technology. It also alleged that province understated the strength of the Coast Tsimshian claim to the Mylor Peninsula
jobs: 800 jobs during construction; 200 jobs during operation
promises: $4B/Year in National Economic Benefits; $17 Billion over projects lifetime

title: Pathways Plus
companies: Suncor, Cenovus, MEG Energy, Imperial Oil, Canadian Natural Resources and ConocoPhilips Canada; Suncor - Owner: Public Investment Fund of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia Sovereign wealth fund of Saudi Arabia. It is among the largest sovereign wealth funds in the world with total estimated assets of $320 billion; Institutional investor: Tradewinds Global Investors, LLC Specialized investment management firm; Owner: Elliott Investment Management LP American hedge fund; Owner: Soroban Capital Partners Hedge fund; Investor: Capital Research Global Investors Los Angeles Headquartered financial services company. It ranks among the world's oldest and largest investment management organizations, with over $2.6 trillion in assets under management; Investor: Tiger Management LLC Julian Robertson's personal hedge fund. Cenovus - An integrated oil company spun off from Encana Corporation in 2009; now owns most of the Canadian business of ConocoPhillips - Canadian corp, Calgary-based. MEG Energy, CAN; Imperial Oil, Canadian, but ExxonMobil, a descendent of Standard Oil, still owns 69.6 percent of our shares; Canadian Natural Resources - "Canadian Natural Resources Limited (CNRL) is one of the largest oil and gas companies operating in Canada today, and Canada’s largest natural gas producer.16 It is also the largest private landowner in Western Canada, second only to the government.18 Its domestic assets include a mix of natural gas, light crude oil, heavy crude oil, bitumen and synthetic crude oil in British Columbia (BC) and Alberta," and ConocoPhilips Canada - ConocoPhillips Canada is the Canadian division of ConocoPhillips, one of the world’s largest independent exploration and production companies based on production and proven reserves.
summary: Nov. 2025:  Pathway Plus is a precondition to the approval of a bitumen pipeline - Alberta and Ottawa reached an agreement that laid out the groundwork for a new bitumen pipeline through British Columbia, part of a suite of changes to Ottawa’s environmental regulatory landscape that charts a course for the country’s energy sector over the coming decades; Woodward analysis: "The Pathways Alliance wants to build a $16.5-billion project to pipe carbon dioxide captured from 13 oilsands sites in northern Alberta to an underground storage site south of Cold Lake, Alberta, through 600 kilometres of pipelines. The group includes the country’s oilsands majors (Suncor, Cenovus, MEG Energy, Imperial Oil, Canadian Natural Resources and ConocoPhilips Canada) representing 95 per cent of oilsands production. Formed in 2021, the group says it is committed to net-zero emissions by 2050. Whether the project makes its way onto the federal government’s major projects list is likely to be a result of the “grand-bargain” being negotiated  between the Carney government and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith. Details are thin, but Carney seems willing to fast-track a new oil pipeline to the West Coast, if the oil can be “decarbonized”  — though climate experts are clear that decarbonized oil does not exist in reality. Still, there are major obstacles. First is that the business case is going up in smokewithout long-term federal subsidies. Second, the companies in the alliance have in recent years raked in record profits, and have not begun work on the project, offering credence to critics’ concerns the project was pitched as a delay tactic  to forestall stricter environmental regulations. Third, carbon capture facilities routinely fail to meet  their stated performance targets. Fourth, the Pathways Alliance project faces opposition  from the local Athabasca Chipewayan First Nation. However, despite its many problems, the political rationale is tantalizing for the federal government. Resetting relationships with Alberta, while politically threading the needle between demonstrating a commitment to the oil and gas sector and reducing emissions, appears to be a major goal for Carney’s government. It also fits the PMO’s understanding of clean technologies, and aligns with a stated criteria for major projects “having clean growth potential, such as the use of clean technologies.”
permitting_stage: Planning Stage: Developing regulatory and technical reports; MOU signed Nov. 2025 between Canada and Alberta to move this and similar projects along.
commodity: Co2 Capture and Export
indigenous_perspective: Feb 2024: Indigenous Nations express concerns as a group regarding the pipeline (Heart Lake First Nation, Beaver Lake Cree Nation, Whitefish Lake First Nation, Kehewin Cree Nation, Frog Lake First Nation, Cold Lake First Nations and Onion Lake Cree Nation. Saddle Lake Cree Nation)

title: Arctic Economic and Security Corridor
location: Kitikmeok section (road and port): Inuit, potential overlap with Akaitcho and Tłı̨chǫ Ndè territories at southern end of road (Jericho), Yellowknives Dene First Nation say the project is on their territory. NWT section: Dënéndeh, Tłı̨chǫ Ndè, Michif Piyii (Métis), Akaitcho, Denendeh (Dënësųłinë́ Nëné).
companies: Kitikmeot Corp, Sakku Investments, Yellowknives Dene First Nation, Ticjo Governments, Prov. NWT ;  Stantec partnership with Inuit-owned partnership involving Kitikmeot Corporation and Sakku Investments Corporation, Nunami Stantec Limited-- Stantic providing engineering and environmentl consulting services; Proponent-- West Kitikmeot Resources Corp: Inuit owned-- largest shareholder is the Nunavut Resources Corporation ("NRC"), a wholly-owned subsidiary of the Kitikmeot Inuit Association ("KIA"). "In 2022, a group of investors with substantial Northern and mining experience recapitalized WKR with new investment to accelerate the work of the company."-- website. According to NWT website, the NWT portion of the road/corridor is a collaboration with Indigenous  governments:"Yellowknives Dene First Nation, Tłı̨chǫ Government, and the GNWT... are collaboratively working with the GNWT on a governance structure and ownership models"
summary: Overview: a 900km all-season road with a utilities corrdior will connect the existing highway network to a deepwater port which will be developed at Grays Bay. The Grays Bay port will provide marine access to the Northwest Passage, seen as important for Canadian soveriegnty and defense. The corridor also aims to enhance local trade and development opportunities while providing access to mineral deposits in the Slave Geological Province. The website notes that the corridor will bring resources "north to Arctic tidewater, south to Alberta processing facilities, while goods and hydrocarbons (e.g. LNG, CNG, and diesel) can move north to support NWT and Nunavut industry and communities." It is broken down into two corridor projects: West Kitikmeok  Resources is developing the port and a 230km all-season road running from Jericho to Grays Bay, while GNWT is partnering with Indigenous governments to develop the 400km road running from the terminus of the existing highway in Yellowknife to the border with Nunavut. acc to this News article Kitikmeok Inuit Association has been developing the Grays Bay Road and Port project since 2018; proponency transferred to WKR in 2023. The NWT two-lane all-season gravel road will run through an area where there is currently only a winter road and is expected to cost $1.1B and has been in the works since at least 2019.
permitting_stage: Planning Stage:  MOU signed Nov. 2025; project scope has been submitted to the Nunavut Impact Review Board in February 2025 for a commercial deepwater port, craft harbour & port infrastructure, and a 230-kilometre all-season access road that connects to Jericho Station to initiate the review process; expected project start date is 2035. The NWT portion of the road was expected to also enter the review process this year with a decision anticipated in 2028, but I did not find the project in the registry.
commodity: Provide access to mineral deposits; linked to Taltson hydro project
indigenous_perspective: Kitikmeok section: Inuit ownership. Yellowknives Dene First Nation (YKDFN) says the corridor is largely on their territory, and, therefore, the project must be done with consultation with their community. chief "Betsina says he hasn't been contacted by the federal government about the project. With the downturn of the territory's diamond mines, he says the YKDFN wants a role to play in building this corridor."-- CBC News . NWT section: collaboration between GNWT, Yellowknives Dene First Nation, Tłı̨chǫ Government
analysis: It is a re-brand of the Slave Geological Province Corridor which has been in the works for nearly a decade; concerns have surfaced in the past about impacts to caribou calving grounds. On the Kitikmeok side, 2024 article: In 2022, KIA, the previous proponenet, walk away from the project and it was taken over by WKR. Since then, the project scope has increased to include expanding the capacity of the; the estimated cost has now incerased from $550M to $1B. WKR hopes that 75% will be federally funded while 25% will come from the private sector. WKR owns critical minerals assets which the road would improve access to. "N.W.T. and Nunavut Chamber of Mines president Kenny Ruptash says the corridor could also open up "unlimited" potential for extracting previously inaccessible minerals."-- CBC News. Voices advocating for reframing of arctic security and sovereignty from an Inuit perspective that focus on wellbeing for communities: food security and sovereignty framing in Yellohead brief; from U Ottawa arctic corridors reseearch project-- research  suggests that contrary to the focus on statehood, Inuit understand security in the context of Inuit sovereignty, and their security concerns centre around their lack of knowledge about and lack of agency over shipping traffic and marine areas in their region
financing: Kitikmeok part: 2019: received $21.6M from federal  National Trade Corridors Fund (NTCF); 2024: the Canada Infrastructure Bank provided a $3 million loan, Government of Nunavut has provided CESP and SIP grants. NWT part: received $30M from the National Trade Corridors fund + $10M in funding from the GNWT in 2019
jobs: Construction of the Kitikmeok part of the project will employ 250-400 workers

title: Port of Churchill Plus
location: Cree (5 northeastern manitoban First nations comprise the Nayhenaway Ininisewinuk Cree people jointly proposing an IPCA to protect their shared homelands), Dene (Sayisi Dene is a community made up of Dene who were displaced into the city of Churchill through racist caribou conservation policies), Metis. See map
companies: Arctic Gateway Group (AGG). Companies involved in activity at the port: August 2025: MOU with Montreal-based Fednav to explore options for year-round shipping (i.e., through icebreaking); 2024 MOU with Nukkik Corporation to develop a “Northern Renewable Energy and Infrastructure Corridor” which includes a Kivalliq Hydro-Fibre Link (KHFL) to power communities and mines in Kivalliq; 2024 MOU with Invest Tisdale, the economic development agency for the town of Tisdale, Sask., to have flow of grains/agricultural products; partnership with Hudbay Minerals to ship critical minerals to international markets; partnership with Genesis Fertilizers: companies will source fertilizer inputs from international partners (currently, imports are via US). A Manitoba Crown-Indigenous Corporation board is being convened which will become a partner in project development (Free Press)
summary: The official announcement states "Rooted in partnership with Indigenous Peoples, including in co-operation with Manitoba’s Crown-Indigenous Corporation, this project will upgrade the Port of Churchill and expand trade corridors with an all-weather road, an upgraded rail line, a new energy corridor, and marine ice-breaking capacity. The approach will prioritize Indigenous equity ownership in developing the projects needed to turn the Port of Churchill into a major four‑season and dual-use gateway for the region.  Expanded export capacity in the North through Hudson Bay will contribute to increased and diversified trade with Europe and other partners, while more strongly linking Churchill to the rest of Canada." The port has received growing attention this past year, with proponents asserting that melting sea ice is expanding its shipping season. Discussions around the project have also linked it to Canada's Arctic sovereignty. A couple weeks before the PONIs announcement, Prime Minister Carney touted the potential of the port for exporting agricultural products, critical minerals, and potentially natural gas.  the project could also involve establishing transmission lines to Nunavut. A Manitoba Crown-Indigenous Corporation board is being convened which will become a partner in project development (Free Press)
permitting_stage: Planning Stage/Pre-Cosntruction: Varies largely across specific components of the project - Railway for example is in construction phase already with improvements happening to the wharf as well. MOU signed between Manitoba, Saskatchewan and AGG in November
commodity: Gas and Minerals; Discussions hint at bringing LNG to European markets e.g. Germany where there is a demand (reducing reliance on Russia) (the star). Other nodes: importing inputs for fertilizer manufacturing, grain exports, cirtical mineral exports mined in Manitoba, supplies shipped to mines (e.g. Whale Cove and Rankin Inlet), the proposal comes with potential power lines powering northern mines
analysis: Before receiving attention as a nation-building project, the Port of Churchill was an important source of employment for Churchill and played a critical role in bringing supplies to remote communities in Nunavut. In 2017, a flood left it inoperable, and the US-based corporation that owned it at the time refused to pay for repairs. When ownership was transferred to AGG, they undertook repairs and revitalization with federal and provincial funding. While the port's maintenance is important for local and northern communities, proposals for exporting LNG do not have a proponent associated with it and are seen as questionable in terms of their economic rational (The Star; Exner-Pirot). A recent Yellowhead Institute Special Report which was based on the PRGT and Ksi Lisims LNG projects highlights the current emphasis on "economic reconciliation" while making the important pont that Indigenous equity ownership exposes them to risk. Exner-Pirot points towards the fact that initially, AGG was a consortium 50% owned by private companies who then sold their shares to local and Indigenous communities, which Exner-Pirot asserts was motivated by wanting to minimize "exposure to what was a low-return asset." The railway is built partially on thawing permafrost which is expected to impact its reliability and result in a future need for costly repairs; the permafrost would also pose challenges to constructing an LNG pipeline. Sea ice conditions and how they will impact shipping is also uncertain, but the project could include funding for icebreakers; the current inability to ship year-round has been cited as a key factor preventing grain exports from being profitable and impacting the competitiveness of oil and gas exports compared to all-season ports on the east and west coasts. UBC prof Trevor Heaver also feels it is not financialy feasible, but sees it as a potential "insurance strategy" against potential disruptions at Vancourver or Montreal ports.
financing: Nov. 2025: announcement from Manitoba contributing $51M towards Hudson Bay Railway as part of the Port of Churchill Plus; March, 2025: announcement of $175M for the port and railway over 5 years shared between Transport Canada and Prairies Economic Development Canada. An article dated march 2025 states that the total funding from the federal government over the 6 years leading up was $277M; another source states that over 2018-2025 the port and railway received $415.4M from the federal government and $139.4M from the Manitoba government in funding

analysis: History acc to website: "Originally built in 1968 to address the shipping needs of what was then the Noranda Smelter (Glencore), the Port of Belledune has grown and evolved from the single terminal facility it was then to a four terminal port.  It still provides the gateway through which concentrates move across the globe from its original terminal, but having expanded three times, first in 1995, again in 1998 and again in 2010, the Port of Belledune has assumed a greater role in the economy of the surrounding regions.  It has, in striving to fulfil its mission, become one of the cornerstones of the local economy and provides direct and indirect employment for many local residents." Interesting - are we seeing a new life for circulatory infrastructure as a result of fast tracked extraction? Looks like it's also competing with other ports to be the most important in the region. In their vision statement, they list the commodities they want to prioritize moving: energy; minerals, forestry; agriculture; modular cargo. They also want to be a green hub. Interesting from a regulatory perspective: "The Belledune Port Authority (BPA) is part of the Canada Port Authorities (CPAs). CPAs “are federally incorporated, autonomous, non-share corporations that operate at arm’s length from the federal government. They operate on a commercial basis with a view to being financially self-sufficient. They have been incorporated as shared governance organizations with the objective of advancing the growth and prosperity of the Canadian economy. While CPAs operate on a commercial basis, they also fulfill important public policy objectives, notably navigational safety and environmental protection.”1 - 1Government of Canada, Canadian Port Authorities.

title: Ring of Fire
companies: Wyloo (division of Tattarang investment company), Juno Company, KWG Resources, Canterra Minerals,
summary: The Ring of Fire is a crescent-shaped deposit comtaining critical minerals such as nickel, copper, chromite and platinum. The first mine proposed for the region is Australian junior mining company Wyloo's Eagle's Nest nickel mine. Webequie and Marten Falls agreed to be the proponents for a road leading into the Ring of Fire. The passage of Bill 5, aiming to fast-track mineral development including in the Ring of Fire, has ignited opposition across First Nations. It contains provisions for the establishment of Special Economic Zones, with the Ring of Fire intended to be the first one. Carney has pointed towards exporting minerals from the Ring of Fire as a potential way of meeting his goal of doubling non-US exports. Recently, Ford has also announced funding for Webequie to explore the potential for an additional east-west access road leading to the Ring.
permitting_stage: Assessment Stage (road): The segments of the road leading to the Ring of Fire, Webequie Supply Road and Marten Falls Community Access Road, are nearing completion (see: recent update) of the provincial EA process, while the Northern Road Link lags behind and federal EAs for the segments still need to be completed. Ford expects the WSR provincial EA process to be completed early next year with construction starting in the spring. A federal  regional assessment is also in the works, but Ford has called on Carney to halt the progress and "said progress is being made on an upcoming agreement with the feds that subscribes to Ontario’s red tape-cutting ‘one project, one review’ process" (Northern Ontario Business)
commodity: Critical Minerals
indigenous_perspective: Marten Falls & Webequie proponency in road projects (but this does not mean their commuities have consented), longstanding opposition from Neskantaga, recent action-- Here We Stand camp with Attawapiskat opposing Bills 5 and C-5. Lawsuit from Marten Falls against Ring of Fire development, also: 9 First Nations launch legal challenge against Bill 5 and Bill C-5
analysis: Ford has repeatedly expressed frustration over the requirement for 'duplicative' federal assessment processes delaying development. However, federal impact assessments do not appear to be the sole cause of delays-- the final segment, Northern Road Link, is not expected to complete the provincial EA process until 2028, and after that it may take an additional decade to build-- i.e., completion may take until 2040. Additionally, while Ford has tried to portray Indigenous peoples as getting in the way of development, First Nations are not anti-development-- they are pro consent; furthermore, the Nations that do consent to road construction don't necessarily consent to Ring of Fire mining; and Nations with varying attitudes towards various forms economic development have expressed their opposition Bill 5 for undermining consent and consulation. Marten Falls has applied for an injunction against development in the Ring of Fire; they feel they have not been adequately included in dialogues around this development. They are concerned about impacts to the land of development which they believe will be done without their meaningful involvement in governance. Their decision to pursue court action was triggered by Bill C-5 and Bill 5. Analysis-- there are places elsewhere where there is stronger economic rationale to develop mines and the authors call for smelting domestically mined copper (and capturing the added value rather than exporting to China), but political will to support junior companies and build infrastructure are lacking in these areas. "Rather than wasting time and money on the Ring of Fire, which isn’t going to be developed for 20 years, if ever, the Canadian government would be better off identifying advanced mineral projects and either funding their exploration directly or directly taking stakes in the companies like the United States government is doing."
financing: ON has made recent funding commitments for road construction. In 2014, ON committed $1B in funding to RoF road construction, the federal government was also considering providing funding (up to $1.6B) in 2022.
promises: $22B to Ontario's Economy/30 years: Estimated $60 worth of minerals, $1.5 billion in federal and provincial tax revenue

title: Yukon-B.C Grid Connection
location: Two possible routes (see map on page 5) Likely on Tahltan, Kaska Dena, Territory. Possibly on Teslin Tlingit and Tutchone territory.
companies: Yukon Development Corporation, B.C. and Yukon BC Hydro
summary: This Project Aims to connect Yukon to BC and the North American Power Grid via a 800km high-voltage transmission line. A Yukon Development Report suggests the purpose of the Transmission Line is to gain “Reliable electricity for critical mineral development, continued electrification of homes and transportation, First Nations-owned renewable energy facilities and clean electricity exports to the rest of Canada” The report suggest pre-feasability work projects the project would raise up to $7.6 billion per year in economic growth. analysis: This project would allow certain diesel communities along its path to connect to the grid. This would likely mean some mining projects who currently rely on fossil fuels would benefit from the transmission line as well. Suggestions that proposed mines such as the Casino Copper-Gold Project could potentially move from Natural Gas to Grid Electricity to power their project.  The project however is intended to increase the feasibility of mining in the area with the goal of increasing the extraction of critical minerals in line with NATO defence goals. The Yukon Development Report speaks to electrifying diesel-reliant communities but also how the project might help the energy needs of existing and prospective mines, including, Red Chris Block Cave Expansion, Turnagain, Eskay Creek Revitalization Project, Kitsault Mine, Galore Creek, Schaft Creek, KSM, Premier/Red Mountain, New Polaris, Kutcho, Silvertip, Tenas, Dome Mountain, Ootsa, Kudz Ze Kayah, Casino, MacTung and MacPass.  Banyan Gold Corp also suggested their AurMac project would benefit from this Transmission line.
promises: $7.6B/Year in Economic Growth; The Yukon Development Report suggest the project could raise up to $7.6 billion per year in economic growth. Projected growth includes $ 100 billion in B.C. mineral development,  $81.2 billion in Yukon mineral development, $198.5 billion billion in Renewable energy, and $200 million in Transportation improvements.

title: Dredging Burrard Inlet for TMX Tankers
location: Tsleil Waututh, Squamish, Stó:lō, Hul'qumi'num Treaty Group, Musqueam
companies: Vancouver Fraser Port Authority
summary: Plans are underway, led by the Vancouver Fraser Port Authority, to dredge the navigation channel under Vancouver’s Second Narrows Bridge to allow tankers carrying oil sands bitumen to transit the inlet at fuller loads. At present, Aframax-class tankers cannot leave the Westridge Terminal fully loaded because of draft restrictions. Dredging would remove roughly 20–25 barges of benthic material from the bottom of the channel, enabling these tankers to load more bitumen transported via the TMX pipeline.
permitting_stage: Pre-Planning: Preliminary plans and studies (field studies in May) completed by VFPA
commodity: Oil Sands Bitumen (Overseas Markets)
analysis: Dredging is the excavation of sediment materials from the bottom of waterbodies. The process is known to have significant impacts on benthic ecosystems. Removing sediment destroys habitat for fish, invertebrates, and plants across the aquatic food chain. It also alters sediment dynamics, degrades water quality, and can accelerate shoreline erosion. Because Burrard Inlet is already highly polluted, disturbing the bottom would also re-suspend long-settled contaminants, spreading them through the water column.
financing: The Port of Vancouver would likely finance the dredging. Although it is not a for-profit body, the Port Authority collects rents from terminal operators and reinvests that revenue into infrastructure, allowing its footprint to expand in order to accommodate ever-growing volumes of commodities.
promises: No Claims tied to this directly, however TMX pipeline which will connect to this expected to $9.2B to GDP

companies: Nunavut Nukkiksautiit Corp (wholly-owned subsidiary of the Qikiqtaaluk Corporation the business arm of the Qikiqtani Inuit Association)

These monitoring systems collect river current data, which is a part of the Front-End Engineering and Design (FEED) stage of investigating the potential for community scale hydroelectric project in Iqaluit.”  Environmental Impact Assessment Submission has a proposed submission date of 2029.

indigenous_perspective: NNC says it uses a “Phase Gate” approach to project delivery, where engagement with Rights holders, the public, stakeholders, and partners occurs at every phase. This ensures that project decisions are guided by Rights holders and that all parties have meaningful input. Between each phase, a Decision Gate requires approval from engagement participants and Inuit leadership before proceeding to the next phase.  After receiving approval from Qikiqtaaluk Corporation and the Qikiqtani Inuit Association in 2022 to explore renewable energy solutions for Iqaluit, NNC has conducted multiple community engagement sessions to assess interest and identify preferred technologies and research sites. Ongoing Consultation with Rights Holder, Iqaluit Public and others started in 2023. See List of Consultations. On the Issue of Public Engagement and Seeking Free Prior Informed Consent  the Nunavut Nukkiksautiit Corporation says  “events were held in November 2023 with the local high school, the general public, and key stakeholders to explain the pros and cons of the various renewable energy options, and provide an opportunity for Iqalungmiut to submit ranked ballots, indicating their top 3 choices for development. 76% of submissions formed consensus on the Kugaaluk (McKeand) River South option.”
analysis: These environmental impacts would have serious consequences for Tsleil-Waututh Nation, who have worked for years to restore and maintain traditional harvesting practices in the inlet, including shellfish and herring. Resuspended contaminants threaten both ecological recovery and food sovereignty.
financing: Canadian Northern Economic Development Agency announces 6 million in front end support for engineering and design work for this hydroelectric project. Crown–Indigenous Relations and Northern Affairs Canada contributed $7 million in 2021.
promises: Will replace $60M spent on diseal each year

title: Rehabbing New Westminster Rail Bridge
companies: CN opporated. Used by BNSF, SRY, CN, Amtrak, VIA and Rocky Mountain Rail Tours (outdated source for rail line usage companies from 2002)
summary: Although little information is known about this possible project, this appears to be a bid to replace and/or increase the capacity of a rail bridge between New Westminster and Surrey. The project does not appear to have any formal proposal outlining a specific project and does not appear to be in any formal planning phase nor is it in construction. The current single-track swing rail bridge was originally constructed by the Province of British Columbia in 1904.The bridge is considered a key bottleneck in the regional rail network, and discussions about replacing the bridge to increase capacity have been ongoing. According to a Richmond Chamber of Commerce Report from 2014, The aging New Westminster Rail Bridge creates significant delays for rail cargo moving to and from the Port of Vancouver. A key issue is the conflict between rail and marine traffic, as the swing bridge gives priority to vessels, preventing trains from crossing for long periods each day. The bridge’s limited capacity already causes delays for all users and is expected to become an even greater source of delays in the future. Brad Bodner the now retired Director of Business Development at CN told a 2018 Standing Committee on Transport, Infrastructure and Communities that the “It is regularly used by six railways, three freight and three passenger, totalling some 40 trains per day.” Please note that it is possible that this project could refer to the Seismic Retrofit of the bridge that happened in early the 2020s although this seems unlikely.
permitting_stage: No Specific Project Proposed
commodity: Rail bridge that brings numerous commodities by six railways to the Port of Vancouver
indigenous_perspective: TBD
analysis: The current span of the bridge limits the size of vessel that can traverse the fraser river according to a Richmond Chamber of Commerce Report from 2014. Replacing the bridge could therefore potentially increase the size of vessels able to traverse the river. Replacement or increasing its capacity could further mean an increase of If the capacity of the bridge to increase the 'flow of commodities’ to the port of Vancouver. Replacement or rehabilitation of the Bridge could potentially increase the capacity for passenger rail according to a Report by citizen transportation advocacy group Transport Action Canada.
promises: No specific economic promises found, Allusions to a decrease in the bottleneck of train traffic on the way to the port of Vancouver presumably helping to increase capacity of goods transported

title: Western Collaboration on the Advancement of Economic Corridors [MOU]
companies: Provincial level governments
summary: In May of 2025, Western premiers met and identifed a need for “Nation-building infrastructure in Western Canada, such as highways, railways, ports, airports, pipelines, nuclear projects and electricity transmission systems” to bring "oil and gas, liquefied natural gas, uranium, electricity and hydroelectricity to Canadian and world markets" (statement). Specifically, they have put forward the idea of an “economic corridor linking Prince Rupert and B.C.’s coast to the Port of Churchill in Manitoba and Grays Bay in Nunavut,” which premiers and Carney all seem to resonate on. It could also include a northwest oil pipeline, but there is a lack of support from BC. Premiers also called for electrical transmission infrastructure to power critical mineral devleopment in remote areas (statement).
permitting_stage: Concept Stage/MOU: Focus on projects which are "shovel-ready"; "In the concept stage" according to the Globe and Mail leaked list; “Reid Skelton-Morven, a Prince Rupert councillor and member of the Ts’msyen and Nisga’a Nations, said a $750-million expansion of the city’s port is well underway.”-- Vancouver Sun
commodity: Infrastructure: Energy, Minerals, Oil and Gas, Transport,
indigenous_perspective: TBD (early stages)
analysis: Western premiers called on support from Carney, particularly to connect ports on the West coast to the coast of Hudson's Bay (Global News). Carney has already established a First Mile Fund for "building transportation networks to connect energy extraction sites to rail lines and roads" in response to these calls (release).
